title: Sandstorm Prep
desc: The horizon is brown. You have two hours before it arrives.

steps.text:
The wall of brown on the horizon isn't a wall. It's a sandstorm — three miles high and moving fast. A Mipedian weather-reader takes one look and starts shouting.

"Two hours! Maybe less! Everything that isn't stone needs to be covered, tied down, or underground!"

The dew farm is the most vulnerable. The silk nets are delicate — a sandstorm will shred them in minutes. The water channels will fill with grit. The cistern will be contaminated if the cover isn't sealed.

Every worker is mobilized. You're handed a coil of rope and pointed at the nets.

options.label: Take down the nets — fold and store them
Seal the cistern first — protect the water

steps.text:
The nets come down in sections. You unhook the top anchors and the silk cascades like a waterfall of spider-web, pooling on the ground in shimmering heaps. Folding is an art — the silk tangles if you rush, and tangled silk is ruined silk.

You fold with a partner, walking toward each other with the edges, meeting in the middle, folding again. Six nets. Each one takes four minutes. The brown wall is visibly closer by the time you finish the last one.

The nets go into stone lockers — sealed boxes built into the ground, sand-proof. You slam the last locker shut as the first gusts hit. Sand stings your face. The air turns brown.

You dive into the storm shelter — a low stone building with thick walls and a door that seals with a crossbar. Inside, twelve workers huddle together while the desert screams.

Two hours later, the storm passes. The nets are intact in their lockers. The channels are full of sand, but that's a tomorrow problem. Today, the silk survived.

The cistern cover is a stone disc, heavy as a cart, seated in a groove around the cistern's rim. In theory, it seals perfectly. In practice, sand finds every gap.

You help three workers drag the cover into position. It scrapes against the groove — not seating flush. Sand has accumulated in the seal. You clear it with a brush, grain by grain, while the brown wall creeps closer.

The cover seats with a thunk. You seal the edges with wet clay — it'll harden before the storm hits and create an airtight bond. You press it into every crack, every seam, every possible entry point for sand.

The storm arrives while you're finishing the last seam. Sand blasts against the cistern cover. You hear it hissing against the stone as you run for the shelter.

After the storm, you check the cistern. The clay held. The water inside is clear, clean, untouched. In the desert, that's not just good work. That's survival.

options.label: Check the water and breathe with relief
